docs.title: Prompt
docs.text:
User: As a senior Python programmer and software engineer, review the codes in the "reference/autoprof" folder, which contains the current code for the "Autoprof" package. 

You can find the documentation for AutoProf here: https://autoprof.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ and the publication here: https://academic.oup.com/mnras/article/508/2/1870/6373481?login=false 

In particular, I'd like you to focus on the Python codes in the "src/autoprof/pipeline_steps" folder, especially the "Isophote_Initialize.py", "Isophote_Fit.py", "Isophote_Extract.py", and "Ellipse_Model.py". These functions correspond to isoster's main functions, but with some different implementations. 

Thoroughly and meticulously review the AutoProf code, compare with isoster. And I'd like you to summarize the finding for: 

1. The difference of core algorithms between AutoProf and Isoster. 
2. An analysis of the pros and cons of these different choices. 
3. Whether it is feasible to implement the different algorithms in Isoster.

docs.title: Code Review: AutoProf vs Isoster - Core Algorithm Comparison
docs.text: Based on my thorough review of the AutoProf codebase (particularly the pipeline_steps folder) and comparison with Isoster, here are my findings:

docs.title: A. Initialization Approach
docs.text:
AutoProf (Isophote_Initialize.py): - Uses FFT-based global fitting to determine initial ellipticity and PA - Extracts circular isophotes (eps=0) at growing radii until background level is reached - Uses FFT coefficient phase (specifically the 2nd coefficient) to determine position angle - Optimizes ellipticity by minimizing: (IQR + |FFT₂|) / median_flux - Performs two-pass grid search (coarse then fine) followed by Nelder-Mead optimization - Computes errors by fitting multiple isophotes within ±1 PSF of the best-fit radius - Uses iqr (inter-quartile range) for robust statistics instead of standard deviation

Isoster: - No explicit initialization routine - relies on user-provided or default initial geometry - Starts fitting immediately from a specified SMA with provided eps/pa values - No global FFT-based parameter estimation

Key Difference: AutoProf performs sophisticated global analysis before local fitting; Isoster assumes reasonable initial conditions are provided externally.

docs.title: B. Isophote Fitting Philosophy
docs.text:
AutoProf (Isophote_Fit_FFT_Robust): - Simultaneous multi-isophote optimization with regularization - Fits ALL isophotes together in an iterative stochastic optimization loop - Uses FFT coefficients as the optimization target (minimize 2nd harmonic power) - Regularization term couples adjacent isophotes to enforce smooth profiles - Loss function: L = |FFT₂|/flux + λ·∑(differences with neighbors) - Random perturbation sampling (5-10 samples per iteration depending on iteration count) - Cycles between optimizing PA, ellipticity, superellipse C parameter, and Fourier modes - Uses upper quantile clipping (0.85 or 0.9) when computing FFTs for robustness - Iterates until convergence (no updates for N consecutive iterations)

Isoster (fitting.py): - Sequential single-isophote fitting (fits one isophote at a time, independently) - Uses harmonic fitting (1st and 2nd harmonics) via least squares - Minimizes harmonic amplitudes iteratively via Jedrzejewski (1987) geometry updates - No explicit regularization between adjacent isophotes - Convergence when |max_harmonic| < conver * rms (local criterion only) - Optional central regularization penalty for low-SMA stability (recently added)

Key Difference: AutoProf uses global coupled optimization with FFT loss; Isoster uses local harmonic minimization (photutils-style).

docs.title: C. Sampling Strategy
docs.text:
**AutoProf (_iso_extract in SharedFunctions.py):** 

# Key sampling code:
theta = np.linspace(0, 2π(1 - 1/N), N)
theta = arctan((1-ε)·tan(θ)) + π·(cos(θ) < 0)  # ← Convert to ellipse coords
- Samples **uniformly in φ** (position angle), NOT in eccentric anomaly - Usesarctan((1-ε)·tan(θ))transformation which IS the eccentric anomaly conversion! - Applies **Lanczos or bicubic interpolation** for small radii (< 30 pixels default) - Uses **integer pixel sampling** for large radii (faster) - Supports **Fourier modes** and **superellipse** perturbations to the ellipse shape - Has **isoband extraction** (_iso_between`) for low S/N regions (annular averaging)

Isoster (sampling.py): 

# Key sampling code with EA mode:
if use_eccentric_anomaly:
    psi = np.linspace(0, 2π, n_samples)  # Uniform in ψ
    phi = arctan2((1-ε)·sin(ψ), cos(ψ))  # Convert to φ for coordinates
else:
    phi = np.linspace(0, 2π, n_samples)  # Uniform in φ
- Two modes: uniform in ψ (EA) or uniform in φ (traditional) - Always uses bilinear interpolation (scipy.ndimage.map_coordinates with order=1) - Simpler, more consistent sampling (no radius-dependent method switching) - No Fourier mode or superellipse support currently

Key Difference: AutoProf's arctan((1-ε)·tan(θ)) is mathematically equivalent to Isoster's EA mode! But AutoProf always uses this (doesn't have a "regular" mode), while Isoster offers both modes.

docs.title: D. Photometry Extraction
docs.text:
AutoProf (Isophote_Extract.py): - Supports both line sampling (at exact isophote) and band sampling (annular region) - Switches to isoband mode at low S/N (default: 2σ threshold) - Computes Curve of Growth via trapezoid integration with full error propagation - Offers multiple averaging methods: mean, median, mode (smooth mode via kernel density) - Can measure arbitrary Fourier coefficients (not just fitted ones) - Computes direct photometry (pixel sum within ellipse) in addition to integrated COG

Isoster (cog.py, fitting.py): - Only line sampling at exact isophote radius - Elliptical aperture photometry computed separately if requested - COG computed via trapezoid or Simpson integration - Averaging: mean or median only - Harmonic coefficients only computed for fitted orders (3, 4 by default)

Key Difference: AutoProf has much more sophisticated photometry options, especially adaptive isoband extraction for low S/N.

docs.title: E. Model Generation
docs.text:
AutoProf (Ellipse_Model.py): - Logarithmic radial grid for efficiency (more samples near center) - Iterates inward from outer radius (unusual but clever - allows efficient windowing) - Uses proximity tracking to assign each pixel to nearest isophote - Supports Fourier modes and superellipse in model - Can replace masked pixels with model values - Uses UnivariateSpline for interpolating SB, PA, ellipticity profiles

Isoster (model.py): - Iterative geometry refinement (3 iterations) to handle varying PA/eps profiles - Computes elliptical radius using local geometry at each point - Uses interp1d for parameter interpolation - Supports harmonic deviations (a₃, b₃, a₄, b₄) in model reconstruction - More straightforward "for each pixel, find radius, interpolate intensity" approach

Key Difference: AutoProf uses clever proximity tracking with logarithmic sampling; Isoster uses iterative geometric refinement.

docs.title: AutoProf Advantages:
docs.text:
✅ Global consistency: Regularization ensures smooth, physically plausible profiles ✅ Robustness: FFT-based loss with quantile clipping handles outliers well ✅ Adaptive methods: Automatic switching between sampling modes, isoband extraction ✅ Rich photometry: Multiple averaging methods, direct flux measurements, COG with full error propagation ✅ Sophisticated initialization: FFT-based global fitting reduces dependence on initial guesses ✅ Advanced features: Superellipse and Fourier mode fitting out-of-the-box

AutoProf Disadvantages:

❌ Complexity: Much harder to understand, debug, and maintain ❌ Computational cost: Simultaneous optimization is expensive (100-1000+ iterations typical) ❌ Black-box behavior: Stochastic optimization can be unpredictable ❌ Tight coupling: Regularization makes isophotes dependent on neighbors (can propagate errors) ❌ Limited interpretability: FFT loss is less intuitive than harmonic fitting ❌ Less precise geometry: Coupled optimization may not converge to true local minima

docs.title: Isoster Advantages:
docs.text:
✅ Simplicity: Clear, interpretable harmonic fitting following Jedrzejewski (1987) ✅ Speed: Sequential fitting is much faster (~10-30 iterations per isophote) ✅ Predictability: Deterministic optimization with well-defined convergence ✅ Precision: Each isophote independently optimized to local minimum ✅ Modularity: Easier to understand and modify individual components ✅ Well-tested approach: Follows photutils/IRAF ELLIPSE methodology ✅ Numba optimization: Critical paths are JIT-compiled for performance

Isoster Disadvantages:

❌ No global optimization: Each isophote fits independently (can lead to jitter) ❌ Sensitive to initialization: Requires reasonable starting geometry ❌ Less robust to outliers: Relies on sigma clipping rather than robust statistics ❌ Limited photometry modes: No adaptive isoband extraction ❌ No automatic initialization: User must provide or estimate initial parameters ❌ Fewer features: No superellipse or arbitrary Fourier modes yet

docs.title: 3. FEASIBILITY OF IMPLEMENTING AUTOPROF METHODS IN ISOSTER
docs.text: I assess the following components for implementation feasibility:

docs.title: A. FFT-Based Initialization ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆ (Highly Feasible)
docs.text:
Implementation Path: 1. Add new module isoster/initialize.py 2. Implement AutoProf's circular isophote extraction with FFT analysis 3. Use scipy.fftpack.fft to compute FFT coefficients 4. Extract PA from 2nd coefficient phase: pa = -angle(FFT₂)/2 5. Implement two-pass grid search + Nelder-Mead for ellipticity 6. Use scipy.stats.iqr for robust statistics

Benefits: Would make Isoster more automated and user-friendly

Challenges: - Need to handle edge cases (very round/elliptical galaxies) - Should be optional (not all users want automatic initialization) - Would add scipy.stats dependency

Recommendation: ✅ Implement - High value, moderate effort

docs.title: B. Regularized Multi-Isophote Fitting ⭐⭐☆☆☆ (Low Feasibility)
docs.text:
Implementation Path: 1. Would require major architectural changes to fitting.py 2. Replace sequential fitting loop with simultaneous optimization 3. Implement regularization loss terms 4. Add stochastic perturbation sampling logic

Benefits: More robust profiles in some cases

Challenges: - Major code complexity increase - would make Isoster harder to maintain - Slower - defeats Isoster's speed advantage - Less predictable - stochastic optimization is harder to debug - Philosophical conflict - Isoster deliberately chose local optimization

Recommendation: ❌ Do Not Implement - Conflicts with Isoster's design philosophy

docs.title: C. Adaptive Isoband Extraction ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Highly Feasible & Valuable)
docs.text:
Implementation Path: 1. Add new function to sampling.py: extract_isoband_data() 2. Modify extract_isophote_data to accept mode parameter 3. Add config options: isoband_mode, isoband_threshold, isoband_width 4. Automatically switch based on S/N ratio

Benefits: - Dramatically better low-S/N performance (outer isophotes) - Matches AutoProf's adaptive behavior - Relatively simple to implement

Challenges: - Need to carefully handle masked pixels in annular regions - Must maintain compatibility with existing harmonic fitting

Recommendation: ✅ Definitely Implement - High value, medium effort, aligns with Isoster goals

docs.title: D. Robust Statistics (IQR, Mode, Quantile Clipping) ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Highly Feasible)
docs.text:
Implementation Path: 1. Add scipy.stats.iqr as averaging option to config.py 2. Implement smooth_mode function (kernel density peak finding) 3. Add upper quantile clipping option to sigma_clip 4. Modify fitting.py to support additional integrator modes

Benefits: - More robust to outliers than mean/median - Especially valuable for low-S/N regions - Easy to implement

Challenges: - Need to carefully document when to use each method - Mode estimation can be slow (but cache helps)

Recommendation: ✅ Implement - High value, low effort

docs.title: E. Lanczos Interpolation ⭐⭐⭐☆☆ (Moderate Feasibility)
docs.text:
Implementation Path: 1. Add Lanczos kernel implementation to sampling.py 2. Make interpolation method configurable 3. Auto-switch based on radius (like AutoProf)

Benefits: - Better interpolation accuracy for small radii - Reduces subpixel sampling errors

Challenges: - Slower than bilinear (window-based, not vectorized) - Benefits mainly at small radii (< 30 pixels) - Isoster already has EA mode which helps with sampling uniformity

Recommendation: ⚠️ Consider - Medium value, medium effort. Benchmark first to see if speedup from Numba offsets Lanczos cost.

docs.title: F. Direct Photometry Measurements ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆ (Highly Feasible)
docs.text:
Implementation Path: 1. Add function to compute pixel sum within ellipse (not just along isophote) 2. Store in isophote results as total_flux_direct 3. Compare with COG for validation

Benefits: - Useful diagnostic for checking COG accuracy - Enables alternative flux measurement

Challenges: - Need efficient pixel masking for elliptical regions - Should be optional (adds computation time)

Recommendation: ✅ Implement - Medium value, low effort

docs.title: G. Fourier Modes & Superellipse ⭐⭐☆☆☆ (Low-Medium Feasibility)
docs.text:
Implementation Path: 1. Extend sampling to support non-elliptical isophotes 2. Add Fourier mode/superellipse parameters to geometry 3. Modify harmonicfitting to account for shape deviations

Benefits: - Can model boxy/disky isophotes more accurately - Useful for certain galaxy morphologies

Challenges: - Significantly increases complexity of sampling and fitting - Slower - more parameters to fit - Questionable value - most galaxies are well-fit by ellipses + harmonics - Current a₃, b₃, a₄, b₄ harmonics already capture these effects

Recommendation: ❌ Low Priority - These are redundant with Isoster's existing harmonic deviations

docs.title: SUMMARY & RECOMMENDATIONS
docs.text:
High Priority (Should Implement): 1. Adaptive Isoband Extraction - Major improvement for low-S/N regions 2. Robust Statistics (IQR, mode, quantile clipping) - Better outlier handling 3. FFT-Based Initialization - Makes Isoster more user-friendly

Medium Priority (Consider): 4. Direct Photometry - Useful diagnostic, easy to add 5. Lanczos Interpolation - Benchmark performance first

Low Priority (Don't Implement): 6. Regularized Multi-Isophote Fitting - Conflicts with design philosophy 7. Fourier Modes/Superellipse - Redundant with existing harmonics

Overall Assessment:

AutoProf and Isoster represent different design philosophies: - AutoProf: Emphasizes global consistency and robustness through coupled optimization - Isoster: Emphasizes speed, precision, and interpretability through local optimization

The most valuable AutoProf features for Isoster are the adaptive methods (isoband extraction, robust statistics) and initialization, NOT the core fitting algorithm. Isoster's sequential harmonic fitting is simpler, faster, and more precise than AutoProf's stochastic FFT-based approach - this is a feature, not a bug.

The recommended additions would enhance Isoster's robustness and automation without sacrificing its core strengths.

## Code Review: AutoProf vs Isoster - Core Algorithm Comparison

Based on my thorough review of the AutoProf codebase (particularly the `pipeline_steps` folder) and comparison with Isoster, here are my findings:

---

### 1. CORE ALGORITHM DIFFERENCES

#### **A. Initialization Approach**

**AutoProf (`Isophote_Initialize.py`):**
- Uses **FFT-based global fitting** to determine initial ellipticity and PA
- Extracts circular isophotes (eps=0) at growing radii until background level is reached
- Uses **FFT coefficient phase** (specifically the 2nd coefficient) to determine position angle
- Optimizes ellipticity by minimizing: `(IQR + |FFT₂|) / median_flux`
- Performs **two-pass grid search** (coarse then fine) followed by Nelder-Mead optimization
- Computes errors by fitting multiple isophotes within ±1 PSF of the best-fit radius
- Uses `iqr` (inter-quartile range) for robust statistics instead of standard deviation

**Isoster:**
- **No explicit initialization routine** - relies on user-provided or default initial geometry
- Starts fitting immediately from a specified SMA with provided eps/pa values
- No global FFT-based parameter estimation

**Key Difference:** AutoProf performs sophisticated global analysis before local fitting; Isoster assumes reasonable initial conditions are provided externally.

---

#### **B. Isophote Fitting Philosophy**

**AutoProf (`Isophote_Fit_FFT_Robust`):**
- **Simultaneous multi-isophote optimization** with regularization
- Fits ALL isophotes together in an iterative stochastic optimization loop
- Uses **FFT coefficients** as the optimization target (minimize 2nd harmonic power)
- **Regularization term** couples adjacent isophotes to enforce smooth profiles
- Loss function: `L = |FFT₂|/flux + λ·∑(differences with neighbors)`
- Random perturbation sampling (5-10 samples per iteration depending on iteration count)
- Cycles between optimizing PA, ellipticity, superellipse C parameter, and Fourier modes
- Uses **upper quantile clipping** (0.85 or 0.9) when computing FFTs for robustness
- Iterates until convergence (no updates for N consecutive iterations)

**Isoster (`fitting.py`):**
- **Sequential single-isophote fitting** (fits one isophote at a time, independently)
- Uses **harmonic fitting** (1st and 2nd harmonics) via least squares
- Minimizes harmonic amplitudes iteratively via Jedrzejewski (1987) geometry updates
- No explicit regularization between adjacent isophotes
- Convergence when `|max_harmonic| < conver * rms` (local criterion only)
- Optional **central regularization penalty** for low-SMA stability (recently added)

**Key Difference:** AutoProf uses global coupled optimization with FFT loss; Isoster uses local harmonic minimization (photutils-style).

---

#### **C. Sampling Strategy**

**AutoProf (`_iso_extract` in SharedFunctions.py`):**
```python
# Key sampling code:
theta = np.linspace(0, 2π(1 - 1/N), N)
theta = arctan((1-ε)·tan(θ)) + π·(cos(θ) < 0)  # ← Convert to ellipse coords
- Samples uniformly in φ (position angle), NOT in eccentric anomaly - Uses arctan((1-ε)·tan(θ)) transformation which IS the eccentric anomaly conversion! - Applies Lanczos or bicubic interpolation for small radii (< 30 pixels default) - Uses integer pixel sampling for large radii (faster) - Supports Fourier modes and superellipse perturbations to the ellipse shape - Has isoband extraction (_iso_between) for low S/N regions (annular averaging)

Isoster (sampling.py): 

# Key sampling code with EA mode:
if use_eccentric_anomaly:
    psi = np.linspace(0, 2π, n_samples)  # Uniform in ψ
    phi = arctan2((1-ε)·sin(ψ), cos(ψ))  # Convert to φ for coordinates
else:
    phi = np.linspace(0, 2π, n_samples)  # Uniform in φ
- Two modes: uniform in ψ (EA) or uniform in φ (traditional) - Always uses bilinear interpolation (scipy.ndimage.map_coordinates with order=1) - Simpler, more consistent sampling (no radius-dependent method switching) - No Fourier mode or superellipse support currently

Key Difference: AutoProf's arctan((1-ε)·tan(θ)) is mathematically equivalent to Isoster's EA mode! But AutoProf always uses this (doesn't have a "regular" mode), while Isoster offers both modes.

docs.title: E. Model Generation
docs.text:
AutoProf (Ellipse_Model.py): - Logarithmic radial grid for efficiency (more samples near center) - Iterates inward from outer radius (unusual but clever - allows efficient windowing) - Uses proximity tracking to assign each pixel to nearest isophote - Supports Fourier modes and superellipse in model - Can replace masked pixels with model values - Uses UnivariateSpline for interpolating SB, PA, ellipticity profiles

Isoster (model.py): - Iterative geometry refinement (3 iterations) to handle varying PA/eps profiles - Computes elliptical radius using local geometry at each point - Uses interp1d for parameter interpolation - Supports harmonic deviations (a₃, b₃, a₄, b₄) in model reconstruction - More straightforward "for each pixel, find radius, interpolate intensity" approach

Key Difference: AutoProf uses clever proximity tracking with logarithmic sampling; Isoster uses iterative geometric refinement.

docs.title: AutoProf Advantages:
docs.text:
✅ Global consistency: Regularization ensures smooth, physically plausible profiles ✅ Robustness: FFT-based loss with quantile clipping handles outliers well ✅ Adaptive methods: Automatic switching between sampling modes, isoband extraction ✅ Rich photometry: Multiple averaging methods, direct flux measurements, COG with full error propagation ✅ Sophisticated initialization: FFT-based global fitting reduces dependence on initial guesses ✅ Advanced features: Superellipse and Fourier mode fitting out-of-the-box

AutoProf Disadvantages:

❌ Complexity: Much harder to understand, debug, and maintain ❌ Computational cost: Simultaneous optimization is expensive (100-1000+ iterations typical) ❌ Black-box behavior: Stochastic optimization can be unpredictable ❌ Tight coupling: Regularization makes isophotes dependent on neighbors (can propagate errors) ❌ Limited interpretability: FFT loss is less intuitive than harmonic fitting ❌ Less precise geometry: Coupled optimization may not converge to true local minima

docs.title: Isoster Advantages:
docs.text:
✅ Simplicity: Clear, interpretable harmonic fitting following Jedrzejewski (1987) ✅ Speed: Sequential fitting is much faster (~10-30 iterations per isophote) ✅ Predictability: Deterministic optimization with well-defined convergence ✅ Precision: Each isophote independently optimized to local minimum ✅ Modularity: Easier to understand and modify individual components ✅ Well-tested approach: Follows photutils/IRAF ELLIPSE methodology ✅ Numba optimization: Critical paths are JIT-compiled for performance

Isoster Disadvantages:

❌ No global optimization: Each isophote fits independently (can lead to jitter) ❌ Sensitive to initialization: Requires reasonable starting geometry ❌ Less robust to outliers: Relies on sigma clipping rather than robust statistics ❌ Limited photometry modes: No adaptive isoband extraction ❌ No automatic initialization: User must provide or estimate initial parameters ❌ Fewer features: No superellipse or arbitrary Fourier modes yet

docs.title: 3. FEASIBILITY OF IMPLEMENTING AUTOPROF METHODS IN ISOSTER
docs.text: I assess the following components for implementation feasibility:

docs.title: A. FFT-Based Initialization ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆ (Highly Feasible)
docs.text:
Implementation Path: 1. Add new module isoster/initialize.py 2. Implement AutoProf's circular isophote extraction with FFT analysis 3. Use scipy.fftpack.fft to compute FFT coefficients 4. Extract PA from 2nd coefficient phase: pa = -angle(FFT₂)/2 5. Implement two-pass grid search + Nelder-Mead for ellipticity 6. Use scipy.stats.iqr for robust statistics

Benefits: Would make Isoster more automated and user-friendly

Challenges: - Need to handle edge cases (very round/elliptical galaxies) - Should be optional (not all users want automatic initialization) - Would add scipy.stats dependency

Recommendation: ✅ Implement - High value, moderate effort

docs.title: B. Regularized Multi-Isophote Fitting ⭐⭐☆☆☆ (Low Feasibility)
docs.text:
Implementation Path: 1. Would require major architectural changes to fitting.py 2. Replace sequential fitting loop with simultaneous optimization 3. Implement regularization loss terms 4. Add stochastic perturbation sampling logic

Benefits: More robust profiles in some cases

Challenges: - Major code complexity increase - would make Isoster harder to maintain - Slower - defeats Isoster's speed advantage - Less predictable - stochastic optimization is harder to debug - Philosophical conflict - Isoster deliberately chose local optimization

Recommendation: ❌ Do Not Implement - Conflicts with Isoster's design philosophy

docs.title: C. Adaptive Isoband Extraction ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Highly Feasible & Valuable)
docs.text:
Implementation Path: 1. Add new function to sampling.py: extract_isoband_data() 2. Modify extract_isophote_data to accept mode parameter 3. Add config options: isoband_mode, isoband_threshold, isoband_width 4. Automatically switch based on S/N ratio

Benefits: - Dramatically better low-S/N performance (outer isophotes) - Matches AutoProf's adaptive behavior - Relatively simple to implement

Challenges: - Need to carefully handle masked pixels in annular regions - Must maintain compatibility with existing harmonic fitting

Recommendation: ✅ Definitely Implement - High value, medium effort, aligns with Isoster goals

docs.title: D. Robust Statistics (IQR, Mode, Quantile Clipping) ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Highly Feasible)
docs.text:
Implementation Path: 1. Add scipy.stats.iqr as averaging option to config.py 2. Implement smooth_mode function (kernel density peak finding) 3. Add upper quantile clipping option to sigma_clip 4. Modify fitting.py to support additional integrator modes

Benefits: - More robust to outliers than mean/median - Especially valuable for low-S/N regions - Easy to implement

Challenges: - Need to carefully document when to use each method - Mode estimation can be slow (but cache helps)

Recommendation: ✅ Implement - High value, low effort

docs.title: E. Lanczos Interpolation ⭐⭐⭐☆☆ (Moderate Feasibility)
docs.text:
Implementation Path: 1. Add Lanczos kernel implementation to sampling.py 2. Make interpolation method configurable 3. Auto-switch based on radius (like AutoProf)

Benefits: - Better interpolation accuracy for small radii - Reduces subpixel sampling errors

Challenges: - Slower than bilinear (window-based, not vectorized) - Benefits mainly at small radii (< 30 pixels) - Isoster already has EA mode which helps with sampling uniformity

Recommendation: ⚠️ Consider - Medium value, medium effort. Benchmark first to see if speedup from Numba offsets Lanczos cost.

docs.title: F. Direct Photometry Measurements ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆ (Highly Feasible)
docs.text:
Implementation Path: 1. Add function to compute pixel sum within ellipse (not just along isophote) 2. Store in isophote results as total_flux_direct 3. Compare with COG for validation

Benefits: - Useful diagnostic for checking COG accuracy - Enables alternative flux measurement

Challenges: - Need efficient pixel masking for elliptical regions - Should be optional (adds computation time)

Recommendation: ✅ Implement - Medium value, low effort

docs.title: G. Fourier Modes & Superellipse ⭐⭐☆☆☆ (Low-Medium Feasibility)
docs.text:
Implementation Path: 1. Extend sampling to support non-elliptical isophotes 2. Add Fourier mode/superellipse parameters to geometry 3. Modify harmonicfitting to account for shape deviations

Benefits: - Can model boxy/disky isophotes more accurately - Useful for certain galaxy morphologies

Challenges: - Significantly increases complexity of sampling and fitting - Slower - more parameters to fit - Questionable value - most galaxies are well-fit by ellipses + harmonics - Current a₃, b₃, a₄, b₄ harmonics already capture these effects

Recommendation: ❌ Low Priority - These are redundant with Isoster's existing harmonic deviations

docs.title: SUMMARY & RECOMMENDATIONS
docs.text:
High Priority (Should Implement): 1. Adaptive Isoband Extraction - Major improvement for low-S/N regions 2. Robust Statistics (IQR, mode, quantile clipping) - Better outlier handling 3. FFT-Based Initialization - Makes Isoster more user-friendly

Medium Priority (Consider): 4. Direct Photometry - Useful diagnostic, easy to add 5. Lanczos Interpolation - Benchmark performance first

Low Priority (Don't Implement): 6. Regularized Multi-Isophote Fitting - Conflicts with design philosophy 7. Fourier Modes/Superellipse - Redundant with existing harmonics

Overall Assessment:

AutoProf and Isoster represent different design philosophies: - AutoProf: Emphasizes global consistency and robustness through coupled optimization - Isoster: Emphasizes speed, precision, and interpretability through local optimization

The most valuable AutoProf features for Isoster are the adaptive methods (isoband extraction, robust statistics) and initialization, NOT the core fitting algorithm. Isoster's sequential harmonic fitting is simpler, faster, and more precise than AutoProf's stochastic FFT-based approach - this is a feature, not a bug.

The recommended additions would enhance Isoster's robustness and automation without sacrificing its core strengths.

docs.title: AutoProf vs Isoster: Algorithmic Comparison and Feasibility Analysis
Executive Summary
docs.text:
This document provides a detailed comparison between AutoProf's isophote fitting pipeline (from src/autoprof/pipeline_steps) and isoster's core algorithms. The analysis covers initialization, fitting, extraction/photometry, and 2D model reconstruction, followed by an assessment of feasibility for implementing AutoProf-style algorithms as optional modes in isoster.

Key Finding: AutoProf and isoster use fundamentally different algorithmic approaches: - AutoProf: Global FFT-based objectives with explicit radial regularization, stochastic optimization across all isophotes simultaneously - isoster: Local harmonic/gradient-based per-isophote fitting with deterministic Newton-style updates

Both approaches have strengths; porting selected AutoProf ideas into isoster as opt-in modes is feasible and would broaden capabilities without sacrificing isoster's current performance advantages.

docs.title: AutoProf (Isophote_Initialize.py)
docs.text:
Strategy: Derives global position angle and ellipticity from the image itself using Fourier analysis of circular isophotes.

Algorithm: 1. Circular isophote sampling: - Builds sequence of circular radii circ_ellipse_radii starting from 1 pixel, growing geometrically (factor 1.2) until reaching background noise level - Stops when 80th percentile flux drops below (ap_fit_limit+1) * background_noise - For each radius, extracts intensity values along circle using _iso_extract with sigma clipping

  1. Global PA estimation:
  2. Computes FFT of intensity array along each circular isophote
  3. Stores 2nd Fourier coefficient coefs[2] in allphase list
  4. Uses phase of 2nd harmonic from outer 5 radii: phase = (-Angle_Median(np.angle(allphase[-5:])) / 2) % π

  5. This phase directly indicates the major axis direction of the elliptical galaxy

  6. Global ellipticity optimization:

  7. Defines loss function _fitEllip_loss(e, ...):
    • Extracts elliptical isophote values at test ellipticity e and fixed PA p
    • Robustly clips intensities at 85th percentile
    • Computes FFT and combines IQR and |F₂| normalized by median and noise: 
      loss = (IQR/2 + |F₂|/N) / (max(0, median) + noise)

  8. Two-pass optimization:

    • Coarse grid search: test e ∈ [0.05, 0.95] at multiple radii (0.8× to 1.2× reference radius)
    • Fine Nelder-Mead minimization using transformed parameter space (_x_to_eps / _inv_x_to_eps)

  9. Error estimation:

  10. PA error: Standard deviation of phase solutions when varying radius by ±PSF FWHM
  11. Ellipticity error: IQR of best-fit ellipticities from multiple nearby radii

Variants: - Isophote_Initialize_mean: Uses mean/std instead of robust IQR for low-count data - Isophote_Init_Forced: Reads global ellipse from .aux file (forced photometry mode)

docs.title: isoster (driver.fit_image + config)
docs.text:
Strategy: Configuration-driven initialization; no dedicated global FFT-based search.

Algorithm: - Initial geometry from IsosterConfig: - x0, y0: Center coordinates (default: image center) - eps, pa: Initial ellipticity and position angle (from config, not derived) - sma0: Starting semi-major axis (config parameter) - Pipeline: 1. Fit central pixel at (x0, y0) for SMA=0 2. Fit first isophote at sma0 using fit_isophote(image, mask, sma0, start_geometry, cfg) 3. Grow outward/inward from there, using previous isophote geometry as initial guess

Key Conceptual Difference: - AutoProf: Global, image-driven initialization using FFT of circular isophotes; explicitly solves for PA and ellipticity before any radial fitting - isoster: Local, config-driven initialization; relies on good priors and per-isophote corrections to refine geometry

docs.title: AutoProf (Isophote_Fit.py) - FFT/Regularization Based
docs.text:
Main Algorithm: Isophote_Fit_FFT_Robust

  1. Radius selection:
  2. Start near PSF scale; grow geometrically with factor (1 + scale/(1+shrink))
  3. Stop when median intensity drops below ap_fit_limit * background_noise

  4. Enforce minimum ~15 radii; reduce scale if too few found

  5. Parameterization:

  6. For each radius R[i], store dict with:
    • ellip, pa (ellipticity, position angle)
    • Optional C (superellipse exponent; C=2 is pure ellipse)
    • Optional m, Am, Phim (Fourier shape mode indices, amplitudes, phases)

  7. All radii stored in parameters list, coupled together

  8. Loss function _FFT_Robust_loss:

  9. Extract isophote values with _iso_extract (bicubic interpolation, mask support)
  10. Robust clipping at (1 - robust_clip) quantile (default 0.85)
  11. Compute FFT of clipped intensities
  12. Data term: Minimize relative 2nd FFT mode (or average of specified fit_coefs): 
    f2_loss = |F₂| / (N * (max(0, median) + noise/√N))
  13. Regularization term: L1 penalties on differences between neighboring radii:
    • Ellipticity: |Δeps| / (1 - eps_neighbor)
    • PA: |sin(Δpa)| / 0.2
    • Fourier modes: Penalties on Am differences and phase differences
    • Superellipse C: |log₁₀(C₁/C₂)| / 0.1
  14. Total loss: f2_loss * (1 + reg_loss * reg_scale)

  15. This couples all radii and promotes smooth profiles

  16. Optimization:

  17. Stochastic local search across all radii:
    • For each iteration:
    • Shuffle radius indices
    • For each radius i:
      • Evaluate current parameters (baseline)
      • Generate N_perturb random perturbations of one parameter family:
      • Cycle through: ellip, pa, C, then each Am[m], Phim[m]
      • Compute loss for each candidate
      • Accept best if better than baseline
    • Convergence: Stop when no updates for iterstopnochange * (len(R)-1) iterations

  18. Progressive parameter activation:

    • First: fit only (ellip, pa)
    • Then: activate superellipse C if requested
    • Then: activate Fourier modes if requested
    • Optionally initialize Fourier amplitudes from FFT of current isophotes

  19. Error estimation:

  20. For each radius, re-fit with perturbed radii (±5%)
  21. Compute IQR of resulting ellipticities and PA scatter

Other modes: - Isophote_Fit_FFT_mean: Simpler variant for low-S/N data (uses mean/std instead of robust stats) - Photutils_Fit: Thin wrapper around photutils.isophote.Ellipse.fit_image - Isophote_Fit_FixedPhase: Uses fixed global PA/ellip; only selects radii

docs.title: isoster (fitting.py) - Harmonic/Gradient Based, Per-Isophote
docs.text:
Core Algorithm: fit_isophote

  1. Sampling (sampling.extract_isophote_data):
  2. Vectorized sampling along ellipse using compute_ellipse_coords + map_coordinates
  3. Returns IsophoteData(angles, phi, intens, radii):
    • angles: ψ (EA mode) or φ (regular) for harmonic fitting
    • phi: Position angle for geometry updates

  4. Optional eccentric anomaly (EA) mode (Ciambur 2015) for high ellipticity

  5. Per-isophote fitting:

  6. Extract (angles, phi, intens) via extract_isophote_data
  7. Apply sigma clipping in angle-intensity space
  8. Fit 1st and 2nd harmonics via linear least squares: 
    I(θ) = y₀ + A₁sin(θ) + B₁cos(θ) + A₂sin(2θ) + B₂cos(2θ)

  9. Compute radial gradient dI/dsma via compute_gradient:

    • Sample at sma and neighbor(s) (1-2 steps)
    • Use mean/median intensities to estimate gradient and error
    • Includes heuristics to avoid noisy gradients (relative error > maxgerr)

  10. Convergence check:

  11. Build harmonics = [A₁, B₁, A₂, B₂] (zero out if fix_center/pa/eps flags set)
  12. max_amp = max(|A₁|, |B₁|, |A₂|, |B₂|)
  13. Apply central regularization penalty (decaying near center) to discourage large geometry changes
  14. Keep geometry with smallest "effective amplitude" as best_geometry

  15. Converge when |max_amp| < conver * rms after at least minit iterations

  16. Geometry updates (Jedrzejewski-style):

  17. Depending on which harmonic dominates, update (x0, y0, pa, eps) using analytic formulas involving gradient, eps, sma

  18. Includes safeguards for extreme ellipticity and PA flips

  19. Higher-order harmonics (optional):

  20. On convergence, compute a₃, b₃, a₄, b₄, ... either:
    • Sequentially: compute_deviations(angles, intens, sma, gradient, n)
    • Simultaneously: fit_higher_harmonics_simultaneous(angles, intens, sma, gradient, orders)
  21. These are diagnostics for modeling, not used in geometry updates

Coupling across isophotes: - Each SMA uses previous gradient as prior and optional central regularization vs previous geometry - No joint optimization over all SMAs; smoothness arises from per-radius convergence and local inheritance

docs.title: Summary of Fitting Differences
docs.text: Aspect AutoProf isoster Optimization variable Geometry across all isophotes simultaneously Geometry per isophote Objective function FFT amplitude of selected modes + explicit smoothness regularization Harmonic coefficients relative to RMS + gradient constraints Solver Stochastic, coupled, non-linear (simulated-annealing-like) Deterministic, per-radius, Newton-like Geometry models Superellipses + arbitrary Fourier modes built-in Higher harmonics as diagnostics only Coupling Explicit L1 regularization between neighbors Implicit via gradient inheritance and central regularization

docs.title: 1.3 Profile Extraction and Curve of Growth
AutoProf (Isophote_Extract.py)
docs.text:
Input: Geometry from fit (fit R, fit ellip, fit pa, errors, optional Fmodes, C) or forced profile file

Sampling radii: - Flexible sampling styles: 'linear', 'geometric', 'geometric-linear' - Start: ap_sampleinitR or min(1 px, 0.5*PSF) - End: ap_sampleendR or 3 * fitR_max or full image if ap_extractfull

Geometry interpolation: - Use UnivariateSpline on sin(2*pa), cos(2*pa) to handle wrap-around - Ellipticity via spline on _inv_x_to_eps (numerically stable) - Interpolate Fourier modes A_m(R), Phi_m(R) and superellipse C(R) if present

Flux extraction (_Generate_Profile): - For each radius: - Line vs band sampling: - If median flux > ap_isoband_start * background_noise: sample line via _iso_extract - Otherwise: switch to band _iso_between(R±ΔR) with ΔR from ap_isoband_width - Compute: - medflux via configurable method: 'median'|'mean'|'mode' - scatflux via corresponding scatter estimator - isotot via _iso_between(0, R[i]) (direct CoG sum) - Optionally measure Fourier coefficients along isophote (ap_iso_measurecoefs) - Track pixels, maskedpixels

Units and CoG: - If fluxunits == "intensity": SB = medflux / pixscale²; CoG via Fmode_fluxdens_to_fluxsum_errorprop - If fluxunits == "mag": SB in mag/arcsec²; CoG via SBprof_to_COG_errorprop - Output: prof header, prof units, prof data (dictionary of column lists)

docs.title: isoster
docs.text:
Strategy: Fitting and photometry are fused; no separate "extract" stage

  • Each isophote dict contains:
  • sma, x0, y0, eps, pa, intens, rms, intens_err
  • Optional a₃, b₃, a₄, b₄, ... and errors
  • If full_photometry: tflux_e, tflux_c, npix_e, npix_c

CoG: - cog.compute_cog takes isophote list and computes CoG - add_cog_to_isophotes attaches CoG fields back to isophotes - Simpler integration strategy than AutoProf's full SB-profile-based approach

docs.title: Extraction Differences
docs.text: Aspect AutoProf isoster Flexibility Very configurable: sampling mode, band vs line, averaging method, Fourier measurements Streamlined: tied to fitting loop, limited but robust options Output format Explicit SB profile table with units metadata Isophote list with embedded photometry CoG computation Two methods: direct sum + analytic integration from SB profile Single method from isophote list Use case Rich control for experimentation and external pipelines High-throughput, pipeline-embedded extraction

docs.title: AutoProf (Ellipse_Model.py)
docs.text:
Inputs: SB profile + geometry (PA, ellip, optional C, Fmodes) from results["prof data"]

Algorithm: 1. Filter and spline construction: - Filter to radii where SB_e < 0.5 mag (avoid noisy outer regions) - Build splines: sb(R), pa(R), q(R) = 1-ellip(R), optional C(R) - If Fmodes: splines for A_m(R), Phi_m(R); compute Rlimscale ≈ exp(sum |A_m(R_end)|)

  1. Pixel-wise radius computation:
  2. Define ROI around galaxy center

  3. For each pixel: compute (XX, YY), Radius, theta = atan2(YY, XX)

  4. Multi-radius "nearest ellipse" loop:

  5. Iterate radii r in logspace from R[0]/2 to R[-1] (resolution controlled by ap_ellipsemodel_resolution)

  6. For each r:

    • Compute local geometry: pa(r), q(r), C(r)
    • Evaluate superellipse radius scaling: Rscale_SuperEllipse(theta - pa(r), 1-q(r), C(r))
    • If Fmodes: multiply by exp(sum_m A_m(r) * cos(m*(theta + Phi_m(r) - pa(r))))
    • Compute effective elliptical radius: RR = Radius / Rscale
    • For each pixel: if |RR - r| is smaller than current best, update best radius MM and proximity Prox

  7. Intensity assignment:

  8. Convert radius map to SB: MM_sb = sb(MM)
  9. Convert SB to flux: F = 10^(-(SB - zeropoint - 5*log₁₀(pixscale))/2.5)
  10. Truncate: set model to zero for RR > R[-1] (outside outer isophote)
  11. Embed ROI into full-frame array

Outputs: Model FITS file, optional mask-replaced image, diagnostic plots

docs.title: isoster (model.build_isoster_model)
docs.text:
Inputs: List of isophote dicts with sma, x0, y0, eps, pa, intens and optional harmonics

Algorithm: 1. Filter and interpolation: - Filter isophotes with sma > 0; handle central pixel separately - Build 1D interpolators: intens(sma), x0(sma), y0(sma), eps(sma), pa(sma)

  1. Approximate elliptical radius:
  2. Start with approximate radius using outermost geometry

  3. Iterate 2-3 times:

    • Evaluate local geometry at current r_ell
    • Compute r_ell_new = sqrt(x_rot² + (y_rot/(1-eps))²)
    • Stop when max change < 0.1 px

  4. Harmonics (if requested):

  5. For each harmonic order n:
    • Build interpolators for a_n(sma), b_n(sma)
  6. Compute position angle: theta = atan2(y_rot, x_rot)

  7. For each pixel:

    • Evaluate Δr/r = Σ_n [a_n(sma) sin(nθ) + b_n(sma) cos(nθ)]
    • Correct radius: r_corrected = r_ell * (1 - Δr/r)

  8. Intensity assignment:

  9. Interpolate: I = intens_interp(r_effective)
  10. Fill inside smallest isophote with inner boundary intensity
  11. Fill outside largest with fill value

docs.title: Model Differences
docs.text: Aspect AutoProf isoster Geometry models Superellipse + full Fourier distortions Elliptical + harmonic radial corrections Radius assignment Nearest-ellipse search over log-spaced radii Direct computation with iterative refinement Computation Heavier: loop over radius grid with windowed regions Efficient: few global passes Truncation Hard cutoff at outer isophote Smooth fill with configurable value

docs.title: AutoProf FFT Global Init
docs.text:
Pros: - ✅ Robust to poor user guesses: derives PA/ellip from signal-dominated outer isophotes - ✅ Uses phase of F₂ in circular isophotes (physically motivated, relatively stable) - ✅ Built-in error estimates using PSF-scale variations - ✅ Grid + Nelder-Mead is simple and robust for 1D ellipticity search

Cons: - ❌ Extra computational cost upfront (FFT for each radius, multiple loss evaluations) - ❌ Assumes well-behaved elliptical galaxy; may be biased by strong asymmetries, bars, bright structures - ❌ More complexity and hyperparameters (fit limit, PSF, etc.)

docs.title: isoster Config-Based Init
docs.text:
Pros: - ✅ Very simple and fast - ✅ Appropriate when approximate center/geometry already known (e.g., from detection pipeline) - ✅ Less machinery, fewer failure modes

Cons: - ❌ If eps, pa config is poor, first isophote may fail or converge to bad local minimum - ❌ No automatic global check that initial geometry matches image's large-scale structure

docs.title: 2.2 Fitting: FFT Regularized Global vs Local Harmonic-Gradient
AutoProf FFT-Robust Fitting
docs.text:
Pros: - ✅ Global regularization: Explicit smoothness on ellip, pa, C, F-modes → smooth, physically plausible profiles - ✅ Robust to outliers/masks: Robust clipping + regularization; loss normalized by median+noise - ✅ Very flexible geometry: Superellipses and arbitrary Fourier modes built into core solver - ✅ Less sensitive to gradient issues: Doesn't rely directly on dI/dsma; can handle noisy/flat gradients

Cons: - ❌ High algorithmic complexity: Many hyperparameters (scales, regularization, perturb scales, iteration limits) - ❌ Non-deterministic: Random perturbations → harder to reproduce exactly - ❌ Harder to reason about convergence guarantees: Heuristics (no-change iterations) rather than clear Newton steps - ❌ Stochastic search may require tuning or long runtimes for large/complex galaxies

docs.title: isoster Harmonic/Gradient Fitting
docs.text:
Pros: - ✅ Principled, classical algorithm (Jedrzejewski + Ciambur EA) with well-understood behavior - ✅ Analytic relationships: Clear mapping from A₁/B₁/A₂/B₂ to geometry corrections - ✅ Direct use of radial gradient gives physical scaling of corrections - ✅ Deterministic and fast: Linear least squares per isophote; gradient evaluations reuse sampling - ✅ EA-mode support for high ellipticity (Ciambur 2015 best practice) - ✅ Higher harmonics readily available for modeling/QA

Cons: - ❌ More sensitive to gradient quality: When gradient is small/noisy, corrections blow up or stop (handled with heuristics, but delicate) - ❌ Weaker coupling across radii: No explicit joint loss penalizing jagged profiles; smoothness from per-radius convergence and local inheritance - ❌ No built-in superelliptic shapes or arbitrary Fourier distortions; harmonics measured, not fitted jointly with geometry

docs.title: AutoProf
docs.text:
Pros: - ✅ Very rich control surface: Radius sampling, band vs line, sigma clipping, averaging method, Fourier measurements - ✅ Detailed error models and unit metadata - ✅ SB profiles and CoG as explicit tables → convenient for external pipelines, plotting, post-processing - ✅ Band sampling in low-S/N regime helpful to avoid numerical issues

Cons: - ❌ Complexity and many options → larger configuration space; misconfiguration can bias results - ❌ Some features may be overkill for users only needing basic isophote geometry + photometry

docs.title: isoster
docs.text:
Pros: - ✅ Simpler, pipeline-embedded extraction: Intensities and CoG as extension of fitting - ✅ Harder to misconfigure; easier to test and maintain - ✅ Well-aligned with high-throughput use (batch runs)

Cons: - ❌ Less flexible for experimentation: No explicit band vs line, limited averaging statistics, no direct "measure Fourier modes along isophote" separate from fit - ❌ CoG less "exposed" as first-class data product than AutoProf's SB table

docs.title: AutoProf EllipseModel
docs.text:
Pros: - ✅ Strongly geometry-driven: Captures full radial dependence of q(R), pa(R), C(R), F-modes - ✅ Nearest-ellipse assignment across log-spaced radii → resolves structure on multiple scales, smooth transitions - ✅ Naturally incorporates superelliptic and F-mode distortions (boxiness, lopsidedness, etc.) - ✅ Ability to replace masked zones with model values → practical for image cleaning

Cons: - ❌ Heavier computation: Repeated loop over radius grid with windowed regions, per-pixel operations - ❌ Additional complexity from superelliptic and F-mode logic → more chances for subtle bugs - ❌ Thresholding and Rlimscale heuristics may need tuning for very structured galaxies

docs.title: isoster build_isoster_model
docs.text:
Pros: - ✅ Conceptually clean: Define radius in varying-geometry elliptical coordinates, interpolate intensity radially - ✅ Efficient: Small number of global passes; few iterations of local geometry refinement - ✅ Easy to reason about and test - ✅ Already close in spirit to AutoProf but with fewer knobs

Cons: - ❌ Currently limited to harmonics a₃/b₃, a₄/b₄, ... as radial corrections; no direct support for superelliptic shapes (C parameter) - ❌ Lacks explicit use of SB errors to gate radii (could ignore noisy outer isophotes, but that's an extra policy decision)

docs.title: 3. Feasibility of Implementing AutoProf-Style Algorithms in isoster
Overall Assessment: Yes, feasible ✅
docs.text: AutoProf-style algorithms can be implemented as optional modes or alternative backends in isoster without requiring core rewrites. They map cleanly to configuration switches and new solver functions.

docs.title: 3.1 Global FFT-Based Initialization
docs.text:
Feasibility: ✅ High - Low Risk

What to Port: - Add optional initialization backend that: - Builds list of circular radii up to S/N criterion (using IsosterConfig fields for background, noise, PSF FWHM) - Uses isoster's own ellipse sampling (extract_isophote_data with eps=0) to extract isovals - Computes FFT of isovals; derives global PA from phase of F₂ (median of outer radii) - Performs 1D ellipticity search at representative radius using AutoProf-like loss (IQR/median + |F₂|) and Nelder-Mead - Produces init_eps, init_pa, init_R and errors

Integration Points: - In driver.fit_image, before fit_central_pixel and fit_isophote: - If config.init_mode == "fft_global": Run FFT-init routine and override cfg.eps, cfg.pa, potentially cfg.sma0 - Otherwise: Keep current config-based behavior

Complexity / Risk: - Contained: Doesn't modify main isophote solver, only starting geometry - Runtime: FFTs along 10-20 circles are cheap compared to full fitting - Recommendation: Keep clearly optional and off by default

docs.title: 3.2 AutoProf-Style Coupled/Robust Fitting Backend
docs.text:
Feasibility: ✅ Medium-High - Medium Risk

What to Port: - Implement alternative solver that: - Defines shared sample_radii list (similar to AutoProf) using isoster's config for astep / PSF / thresholds - Constructs parameters list per radius with x0, y0, eps, pa (optionally C and harmonics) - Uses isoster's fast sampling to produce intensity arrays along each ellipse, then FFT them - Implements _FFT_Robust_loss (data + regularization) using isoster's parameter structure - Runs stochastic or modern optimizer (coordinate-wise random search, CMA-ES) over (eps, pa, ...) across all radii

Integration Pattern: - Add solver switch in config: - config.solver = "jedrzejewski" (default) or "autoprof_fft" - Implement: - fit_isophote_jedrzejewski (current, renamed) - fit_isophotes_autoprof_style(image, mask, cfg) → returns isophote list in isoster's dict format - driver.fit_image branches on solver and builds isophotes list accordingly

Trade-offs: - ✅ Gains: Option for AutoProf-like behavior (coupled solutions, superellipse/F-modes, robust FFT) - ⚠️ Costs: Complexity, runtime, reproducibility; would need strong tests vs AutoProf before enabling by default

docs.title: 3.3 Richer Extraction / Photometry Options
docs.text:
Feasibility: ✅ High - Low Risk

What to Port: - Extend or parallel existing CoG/photometry stack: - Allow band-based extraction around each SMA (similar to _iso_between vs _iso_extract) - Add config flags for averaging method (mean/median/mode), sigma-clipping iterations, Fourier-mode measurement along isophotes - Return profile object/table similar to AutoProf's prof header/prof units/prof data, while still producing current isophote list

Integration: - Add helper: build_sb_profile(isophotes, image, mask, config) that: - Uses isoster's sampling machinery to re-sample along fitted geometry (for bands) - Matches AutoProf's column semantics - CoG routines could either: - Keep using existing compute_cog, or - Gain alternative "profile-based CoG" more like AutoProf's

Recommendation: High value, low risk - Many users (and QA/benchmarking) would benefit from AutoProf-like SB tables and band sampling

docs.title: 3.4 Superellipse and Fourier-Shape Support in build_isoster_model
docs.text:
Feasibility: ✅ Medium - Medium Risk

What to Port: - Extend isophote dicts to carry optional fields: - C (superellipse exponent vs radius) - Possibly Fmodes (m, Am, Phim) in AutoProf-like representation, or re-interpret existing a_n, b_n harmonics into equivalent radius-scaling factors - Implement analogues of: - Rscale_SuperEllipse(theta, eps, C) - Rscale_Fmodes(theta, A_m, Phi_m, m) - Replace or augment current harmonic Δr/r correction with these radius scalings when flags set

Integration: - build_isoster_model(..., use_superellipse=False, use_fmodes=False, ...): - If disabled: Current behavior unchanged - If enabled: Follow AutoProf-like Rscale construction to derive r_effective for each pixel

Risks / Constraints: - Need consistent definitions of C and F-modes between fit and model (if adopting AutoProf-like fitter) - Important: Don't have to tie this to Jedrzejewski solver; can treat C and F-modes purely as modeling extras at first

docs.title: High Value, Lower Risk (Implement Soon) ✅
docs.text:
1. FFT-based global initialization as optional init_mode:
  2. Clear, encapsulated, improves robustness when config-based guesses are poor

  3. Low risk: Doesn't touch main solver

  4. Richer extraction options & SB profile table:

  5. Many users benefit from AutoProf-like SB tables and band sampling
  6. Low risk: Additive feature, doesn't change existing behavior

docs.title: Higher Complexity, But Feasible as Optional Backends ⚠️
docs.text:
1. Full AutoProf-style coupled FFT fitting:
  2. Feasible but substantial effort
  3. Best implemented as separate solver backend with well-defined inputs/outputs and comprehensive tests vs AutoProf

  4. Keep as opt-in; don't replace default Jedrzejewski solver

  5. Superellipse / F-mode reconstruction in build_isoster_model:

  6. Mostly modeling-layer enhancement
  7. Consider adding once you either:
    • Fit such parameters directly, or
    • Decide on mapping from existing harmonics to these shape descriptors

docs.title: Algorithmic Complementarity
docs.text:
AutoProf and isoster are algorithmically complementary:

  • AutoProf: Emphasizes global FFT-based objectives with strong radial regularization; stochastic optimization across all isophotes simultaneously; very flexible geometry models (superellipses, Fourier modes)
  • isoster: Focuses on fast, deterministic harmonic/gradient-based per-isophote fitting with EA support; efficient modeling; streamlined pipeline

docs.title: Key Differences Summary
docs.text: Component AutoProf Approach isoster Approach Initialization FFT of circular isophotes → global PA/ellip Config-based starting geometry Fitting FFT amplitude + regularization; stochastic global optimization Harmonic least squares + gradient; deterministic per-radius Extraction Very configurable (band/line, averaging, Fourier measurements) Streamlined, pipeline-embedded Modeling Superellipse + F-modes; nearest-ellipse assignment Elliptical + harmonics; direct interpolation

docs.title: Feasibility Conclusion
docs.text: Porting selected AutoProf ideas into isoster as opt-in modes is: - ✅ Technically feasible: Algorithms map cleanly to optional backends - ✅ Low risk for core functionality: Can be implemented as separate solver/init modes without touching existing code - ✅ High value: Would broaden isoster's capabilities (robust initialization, richer extraction, advanced geometry models) without sacrificing current strengths (speed, determinism, EA support)

docs.title: Recommended Implementation Order
docs.text:
1. Phase 1 (Low Risk, High Value):
  2. FFT-based global initialization (init_mode="fft_global")

  3. Richer extraction options and SB profile table output

  4. Phase 2 (Medium Risk, Medium-High Value):

  5. Superellipse / F-mode support in model reconstruction (if needed for science cases)

  6. Phase 3 (Higher Risk, High Value if Needed):

  7. Full AutoProf-style coupled FFT fitting backend (solver="autoprof_fft")
  8. Comprehensive testing and validation vs AutoProf on shared test cases

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• AutoProf: Stone et al. (2021), MNRAS 508, 1870; https://autoprof.readthedocs.io/
  • isoster: ISOSTER (ISOphote on STERoid) - Accelerated Python library for elliptical isophote fitting
  • Jedrzejewski (1987): Classical isophote fitting algorithm using harmonic analysis
  • Ciambur (2015): ApJ 810, 120 - Eccentric anomaly sampling for high-ellipticity isophotes

Document generated: 2026-01-23 Reviewer: Senior Python Programmer / Software Engineer Scope: AutoProf src/autoprof/pipeline_steps vs isoster core pipeline

docs.title: ISOSTER Codebase Review: Efficiency, Weaknesses, and Bugs
docs.text: Date: 2026-01-15 Reviewer: Senior Python Programmer & Software Engineer Scope: Comprehensive code review identifying efficiency improvements, weaknesses, and potential bugs

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
This review identifies efficiency improvements, weaknesses, and potential bugs in the isoster codebase. Many critical issues from previous reviews have been addressed, but several opportunities for improvement remain.

Key Findings: - 1 Critical Bug: PA modulo using π instead of 2π - 5 Efficiency Opportunities: Memory optimization, matrix operations, caching - 7 Code Weaknesses: Error handling, input validation, numerical stability - 6 Potential Bugs: Edge cases and logic issues

docs.title: ✅ Already Optimized (Documented)
docs.text:
The following optimizations have been successfully implemented:

  • EFF-1: Gradient early termination (2% speedup) - fitting.py:490-518
  • EFF-2: Harmonic coefficient reuse (7.7% speedup) - fitting.py:198-243
  • EFF-3: Unused coordinate grid removed - model.py (previous version)
  • EFF-5: PA wrap-around vectorization - fitting.py:52

Total documented speedup: 9.8% (1.294s → 1.167s on benchmark suite)

docs.title: EFF-6: Redundant Data Extraction in compute_gradient()
docs.text:
Location: isoster/fitting.py:450-453, 468-471

Issue: Even with current_data parameter, the function still extracts data at gradient SMA (sma + step) when current_data is provided. The gradient computation requires data at sma + step, which is always extracted fresh, even when geometry hasn't changed.

Current Code: 

if current_data is not None:
    phi_c, intens_c = current_data
else:
    data_c = extract_isophote_data(image, mask, x0, y0, sma, eps, pa, ...)
    # ...

# Always extracts fresh data for gradient SMA
data_g = extract_isophote_data(image, mask, x0, y0, gradient_sma, eps, pa, ...)

Impact: ~15-20% of gradient computation time (called for every isophote iteration)

Fix: Cache gradient SMA data across iterations when geometry hasn't changed significantly: 

# Cache gradient data in fit_isophote() and pass to compute_gradient()
# Only re-extract if geometry changed by more than threshold

Priority: MEDIUM

docs.title: EFF-7: Memory Allocation in compute_aperture_photometry()
docs.text:
Location: isoster/fitting.py:543

Issue: Creates full coordinate grids for every isophote when full_photometry=True: 

y, x = np.mgrid[y_min:y_max, x_min:x_max]

For large images with many isophotes, this causes repeated allocation/deallocation. For a 4096×4096 image with 100 isophotes, this creates ~100 temporary arrays of size (y_max-y_min) × (x_max-x_min).

Impact: - Memory churn and potential cache misses - ~5-10% of aperture photometry time - Significant for large images with many isophotes

Fix Options: 1. Pre-allocate reusable coordinate arrays at driver level 2. Use numba-accelerated version (already identified as opportunity in docs) 3. Use np.ogrid instead of np.mgrid for memory efficiency

Priority: HIGH (easy win)

docs.title: EFF-8: Matrix Operations in Harmonic Fitting
docs.text:
Location: isoster/fitting.py:148-152

Issue: For small sample sizes (<20 points, common at small SMAs), np.linalg.lstsq overhead dominates: 

coeffs, residuals, rank, s = np.linalg.lstsq(A, intensity, rcond=None)
ata_inv = np.linalg.inv(np.dot(A.T, A))

The A.T @ A is computed twice (once internally in lstsq, once explicitly for covariance).

Impact: ~5-10% of harmonic fitting time for small SMAs

Fix: For small systems (n < 20), use direct solve: 

if len(intensity) < 20:
    # Direct solve for small systems
    ATA = A.T @ A
    ATb = A.T @ intensity
    coeffs = np.linalg.solve(ATA, ATb)
    ata_inv = np.linalg.inv(ATA)
else:
    # Use lstsq for larger systems
    coeffs, residuals, rank, s = np.linalg.lstsq(A, intensity, rcond=None)
    ata_inv = np.linalg.inv(np.dot(A.T, A))

Priority: MEDIUM

docs.title: EFF-9: Repeated Interpolation in Model Building
docs.text:
Location: isoster/model.py:128-143

Issue: Creates 4 separate interp1d objects for geometry parameters (x0, y0, eps, pa) that are called repeatedly in loops: 

x0_interp = interp1d(sma_values, [iso['x0'] for iso in sorted_isos], ...)
# Called in loop: x0_local = x0_interp(r_ell_flat).reshape(r_ell.shape)

interp1d has function call overhead. For linear interpolation, np.interp is faster.

Impact: ~10-15% of model building time

Fix: Use np.interp for linear interpolation: 

# Instead of interp1d, use np.interp directly
x0_local = np.interp(r_ell_flat, sma_values, x0_values).reshape(r_ell.shape)

Priority: MEDIUM

docs.title: EFF-10: Inefficient Elliptical Radius Iteration
docs.text:
Location: isoster/model.py:163-187

Issue: Iterates up to 3 times to find elliptical radius using local geometry: 

for iteration in range(max_iterations):
    # Get local geometry at current radius estimate
    # Recompute elliptical radius with local geometry
    # Check convergence

Could converge faster with better initial guess (use previous pixel's radius).

Impact: ~10-15% of model building time

Fix: Use previous pixel's radius as initial guess for adjacent pixels (spatial coherence).

Priority: LOW

docs.title: WEAK-1: Position Angle Modulo Bug (HIGH PRIORITY)
docs.text:
Location: isoster/fitting.py:805, 810

Issue: Position angle is wrapped to [0, π) instead of [0, 2π): 

pa = (pa + (max_amp * 2.0 * (1.0 - eps) / sma / gradient / denom)) % np.pi
# ...
pa = (pa + np.pi/2) % np.pi

This loses information about ellipse orientation (can't distinguish 0° from 180°). Position angle should be in [0, 2π) to fully specify ellipse orientation.

Impact: - Incorrect PA values in results - Potential geometry errors in downstream analysis - Inconsistent with astronomical conventions

Fix: Change to % (2.0 * np.pi): 

pa = (pa + ...) % (2.0 * np.pi)
pa = (pa + np.pi/2) % (2.0 * np.pi)

Priority: P0 (Critical - Fix Before Next Release)

docs.title: WEAK-2: Inconsistent Error Handling
docs.text:
Location: Multiple files

Issue: Mix of error handling patterns: - Some functions return (0.0, 0.0, 0.0, 0.0) on error (compute_parameter_errors, compute_deviations) - Some return np.nan (fit_central_pixel) - Some raise exceptions (IsosterConfig validation) - Some use warnings (fitting.py:268-272)

Impact: - Unpredictable behavior for downstream code - Difficult to distinguish between "no data" and "error" - Silent failures can hide bugs

Fix: Standardize on exception-based error handling: - Use specific exception types (ValueError, RuntimeError, etc.) - Only return default values when semantically meaningful (e.g., np.nan for missing data) - Document error conditions clearly

Priority: P1

docs.title: WEAK-3: Magic Numbers Not Configurable
docs.text:
Location: Multiple files

Issues: - 64 minimum samples (sampling.py:135) - hardcoded sampling density - 0.8 gradient fallback multiplier (fitting.py:456, 521) - arbitrary fallback value - 1e-10 regularization cutoff (fitting.py:213, 804) - numerical threshold - 0.95 maximum ellipticity (fitting.py:807, 809) - hard limit - 0.5 minimum SMA limit (driver.py:149) - hardcoded lower bound

Impact: Hard to tune for different use cases (high-SNR vs low-SNR, different galaxy types)

Fix: Add to IsosterConfig with sensible defaults: 

min_samples: int = Field(64, description="Minimum number of sampling points per isophote")
gradient_fallback_mult: float = Field(0.8, description="Multiplier for gradient fallback")
reg_cutoff: float = Field(1e-10, description="Regularization cutoff threshold")
max_ellipticity: float = Field(0.95, description="Maximum allowed ellipticity")
min_sma_limit: float = Field(0.5, description="Minimum SMA limit in pixels")

Priority: P1

docs.title: WEAK-4: Missing Input Validation
docs.text:
Location: isoster/numba_kernels.py, isoster/sampling.py

Issues: - compute_ellipse_coords() doesn't validate n_samples > 0 - extract_isophote_data() doesn't validate image shape - No bounds checking for eps near 1.0 in EA conversion - No validation that sma > 0

Impact: Runtime errors with unclear messages (e.g., ZeroDivisionError: division by zero)

Fix: Add input validation with clear error messages: 

def compute_ellipse_coords(n_samples, sma, eps, pa, x0, y0, use_ea):
    if n_samples <= 0:
        raise ValueError(f"n_samples must be > 0, got {n_samples}")
    if sma <= 0:
        raise ValueError(f"sma must be > 0, got {sma}")
    if not (0 <= eps < 1):
        raise ValueError(f"eps must be in [0, 1), got {eps}")
    # ...

Priority: P1

docs.title: WEAK-5: Numerical Stability at Edge Cases
docs.text:
Location: Multiple files

Issues:

  1. EA conversion unstable when eps → 1.0 (sampling.py:44-49): 

    position_angle = np.arctan2((1.0 - eps) * np.sin(psi), np.cos(psi))
    When eps ≈ 1.0, (1.0 - eps) * np.sin(psi) becomes very small, leading to numerical instability.

  2. Division by (1.0 - eps) when eps ≈ 1.0 (model.py:181): 

    r_ell_new = np.sqrt(x_rot**2 + (y_rot / (1.0 - eps_safe))**2)
    Even with clipping to 0.99, very small denominators can cause issues.

  3. Regularization underflow for very small SMA (fitting.py:37-39): 

    lambda_sma = config.central_reg_strength * np.exp(-(sma / config.central_reg_sma_threshold)**2)
    For sma << threshold, this can underflow to zero.

Impact: NaN/Inf values, convergence failures, incorrect results

Fix: Add epsilon checks and clamping: 

# Clamp eps away from 1.0
eps_safe = np.clip(eps, 0, 0.999)

# Add minimum threshold for regularization
lambda_sma = max(lambda_sma, 1e-12) if lambda_sma > 0 else 0.0

Priority: P1

docs.title: WEAK-6: Inefficient Config Access Pattern
docs.text:
Location: isoster/fitting.py:442-445

Issue: Repeated hasattr() checks in hot path: 

step = config.astep if hasattr(config, 'astep') else config['astep']
linear_growth = config.linear_growth if hasattr(config, 'linear_growth') else config['linear_growth']

Config should be consistently either object or dict. This pattern suggests unclear API design.

Impact: - Minor performance overhead (attribute lookup vs dict access) - Code complexity and maintenance burden

Fix: Normalize config to object at function entry: 

def fit_isophote(image, mask, sma, start_geometry, config, ...):
    # Normalize config once at entry
    if isinstance(config, dict):
        cfg = IsosterConfig(**config)
    elif isinstance(config, IsosterConfig):
        cfg = config
    else:
        raise TypeError(f"config must be dict or IsosterConfig, got {type(config)}")

    # Use cfg directly throughout (no hasattr checks needed)
    step = cfg.astep
    linear_growth = cfg.linear_growth

Priority: P2

docs.title: WEAK-7: Gradient Computation Logic Issue
docs.text:
Location: isoster/fitting.py:520-522

Issue: When gradient is suspicious, it's replaced but gradient_error is set to None: 

if gradient >= (previous_gradient / 3.0):
    gradient = previous_gradient * 0.8
    gradient_error = None

This doesn't update previous_gradient, and the error propagation is unclear. Downstream code may not handle gradient_error = None correctly.

Impact: - Incorrect error propagation - Potential issues in error computation downstream

Fix: Either: 1. Update previous_gradient to the fallback value 2. Propagate error estimate (use previous error scaled by 0.8) 3. Document the behavior clearly

Priority: P2

docs.title: BUG-1: PA Modulo Using π Instead of 2π (CONFIRMED)
docs.text:
Location: isoster/fitting.py:805, 810

Status: Still present in current codebase

Severity: HIGH - Causes incorrect position angle values

Current Code: 

pa = (pa + (max_amp * 2.0 * (1.0 - eps) / sma / gradient / denom)) % np.pi
# ...
pa = (pa + np.pi/2) % np.pi

Problem: Position angle wrapped to [0, π) loses orientation information.

Fix: Change to % (2.0 * np.pi): 

pa = (pa + ...) % (2.0 * np.pi)
pa = (pa + np.pi/2) % (2.0 * np.pi)

Priority: P0 (Critical)

docs.title: BUG-2: Gradient Error Not Updated on Fallback
docs.text:
Location: isoster/fitting.py:520-522

Issue: When gradient is replaced with fallback value, gradient_error is set to None but this may not be handled correctly downstream. The previous_gradient is not updated, which could cause issues in subsequent iterations.

Severity: MEDIUM - May cause incorrect error estimates

Fix: Update previous_gradient or propagate error estimate: 

if gradient >= (previous_gradient / 3.0):
    gradient = previous_gradient * 0.8
    gradient_error = previous_gradient_error * 0.8 if previous_gradient_error else None
    previous_gradient = gradient  # Update for next iteration

Priority: P1

docs.title: BUG-3: Potential Index Out of Bounds
docs.text:
Location: isoster/driver.py:21, 24

Issue: No bounds checking when accessing central pixel: 

val = image[int(np.round(y0)), int(np.round(x0))]

If x0 or y0 are outside image bounds, this will raise IndexError.

Severity: MEDIUM - Will crash on edge cases

Fix: Add bounds checking: 

x_idx = int(np.round(x0))
y_idx = int(np.round(y0))
if not (0 <= y_idx < image.shape[0] and 0 <= x_idx < image.shape[1]):
    return {
        # ... error result with stop_code=-1
    }
val = image[y_idx, x_idx]

Priority: P0 (Critical for robustness)

docs.title: BUG-4: Division by Zero in Model Building
docs.text:
Location: isoster/model.py:181

Issue: Even with eps_safe = np.clip(eps_local, 0, 0.99), if eps_local values are exactly 1.0 (from bad data), the clipping might not catch all edge cases. The division y_rot / (1.0 - eps_safe) could still be problematic.

Severity: LOW - Already has clipping, but could be more robust

Fix: Add explicit check and use tighter clipping: 

eps_safe = np.clip(eps_local, 0, 0.999)  # Tighter bound
# Or add explicit check:
denom = 1.0 - eps_safe
denom = np.maximum(denom, 1e-6)  # Ensure minimum denominator
r_ell_new = np.sqrt(x_rot**2 + (y_rot / denom)**2)

Priority: P2

docs.title: BUG-5: Inconsistent Return Types
docs.text:
Location: isoster/fitting.py:compute_gradient()

Issue: Returns (gradient, gradient_error) where gradient_error can be None, but callers may not always check. Type hints are missing, making it unclear.

Severity: LOW - Currently handled, but fragile

Fix: Use Optional[float] type hints and ensure all callers handle None: 

from typing import Tuple, Optional

def compute_gradient(...) -> Tuple[float, Optional[float]]:
    # ...

Priority: P2

docs.title: BUG-6: Potential Memory Leak in Model Building
docs.text:
Location: isoster/model.py:160-187

Issue: Creates large temporary arrays in iteration loop: 

for iteration in range(max_iterations):
    r_ell_flat = r_ell.ravel()  # Creates view, but...
    x0_local = x0_interp(r_ell_flat).reshape(r_ell.shape)  # Creates new array
    # ... more array creation

For large images, these arrays may not be garbage collected promptly.

Severity: LOW - Python GC should handle, but could be optimized

Fix: Explicitly delete large arrays or use smaller working arrays: 

# Use smaller working arrays or explicit cleanup
del r_ell_flat, x0_local, y0_local, eps_local, pa_local

Priority: P3

docs.title: QUAL-1: Type Hints Incomplete
docs.text:
Location: Most functions

Issue: Many functions lack type hints, making it harder to: - Catch errors with type checkers (mypy, pyright) - Understand function signatures - Use with IDEs that support type checking

Fix: Add comprehensive type hints: 

from typing import Tuple, Optional, Dict, List, Union
from numpy.typing import NDArray

def fit_isophote(
    image: NDArray[np.floating],
    mask: Optional[NDArray[np.bool_]],
    sma: float,
    start_geometry: Dict[str, float],
    config: Union[Dict, IsosterConfig],
    going_inwards: bool = False,
    previous_geometry: Optional[Dict[str, float]] = None
) -> Dict[str, Union[float, int]]:
    # ...

Priority: P2

docs.title: QUAL-2: Docstring Inconsistencies
docs.text:
Location: Multiple files

Issue: Some functions have detailed docstrings (NumPy style), others have minimal or missing ones: - fit_isophote(): Comprehensive docstring ✓ - harmonic_function(): Missing docstring ✗ - compute_gradient(): Good docstring ✓ - extract_isophote_data(): Good docstring ✓

Fix: Standardize docstring format (NumPy style) across all public functions: 

def function_name(param1: type, param2: type) -> return_type:
    """
    Brief description.

    Longer description if needed.

    Parameters
    ----------
    param1 : type
        Description of param1.
    param2 : type
        Description of param2.

    Returns
    -------
    return_type
        Description of return value.

    Raises
    ------
    ValueError
        When invalid input is provided.

    Notes
    -----
    Additional notes if needed.
    """

Priority: P3

docs.title: QUAL-3: Test Coverage Gaps
docs.text:
Location: Edge cases

Issue: Some edge cases not covered: - eps = 0.999 (very high ellipticity) - sma < 0.5 (very small SMA) - Empty images (image.shape = (0, 0)) - All-masked images - x0, y0 outside image bounds - gradient = None propagation

Current Coverage: 63% overall (from CODE_REVIEW.md)

Fix: Add comprehensive edge case tests: 

def test_high_ellipticity():
    """Test fitting with eps = 0.999"""
    # ...

def test_small_sma():
    """Test fitting with sma < 0.5"""
    # ...

def test_empty_image():
    """Test with empty image"""
    # ...

Priority: P2

docs.title: Immediate (P0 - Fix Before Next Release)
docs.text:
1. BUG-1: Fix PA modulo to use 2π instead of π (fitting.py:805, 810)
  2. BUG-3: Add bounds checking in fit_central_pixel() (driver.py:21, 24)

docs.title: High Priority (P1 - Next Sprint)
docs.text:
1. EFF-7: Optimize compute_aperture_photometry() memory usage
  2. EFF-8: Optimize small matrix operations in harmonic fitting
  3. WEAK-3: Expose magic numbers as config parameters
  4. WEAK-4: Add input validation with clear error messages
  5. WEAK-5: Improve numerical stability at edge cases
  6. BUG-2: Fix gradient error propagation on fallback

docs.title: Medium Priority (P2 - Future Releases)
docs.text:
1. EFF-6: Cache gradient SMA data across iterations
  2. EFF-9: Optimize interpolation in model building
  3. WEAK-2: Standardize error handling patterns
  4. WEAK-6: Simplify config access pattern
  5. WEAK-7: Fix gradient computation logic

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text:
The isoster codebase is generally well-structured with good vectorization and performance optimizations. However, there are several critical issues that should be addressed:

  1. Critical Bug: PA modulo bug must be fixed before next release
  2. Efficiency: Several easy wins for memory and computation optimization
  3. Robustness: Input validation and error handling need improvement
  4. Code Quality: Type hints and documentation need standardization

The codebase shows evidence of active maintenance and improvement (many issues from previous reviews have been resolved). The remaining issues are mostly edge cases and code quality improvements that will enhance robustness and maintainability.

  • Numba optimization: docs/development/NUMBA_CODE_REVIEW.md
